questions.stem: Which of the following is an example of a social engineering attack?
questions.explanation: Phishing is a social engineering attack because it manipulates people rather than exploiting technical vulnerabilities. The attacker sends a fake email to trick the victim into revealing credentials.
questions.tip: Social engineering = attacking behaviour. Technical attacks = exploiting software or hardware weaknesses. Firewalls are a defence, not an attack.

questions.stem: A user types the correct URL for their bank's website but is redirected to a fraudulent site without clicking any link. Which attack has occurred?
questions.explanation: Pharming redirects users to fraudulent websites even when they type the correct address — by corrupting DNS records or modifying the hosts file. No deceptive link needs to be clicked.
questions.tip: The key distinction: phishing requires the victim to click a fake link. Pharming redirects them even when they type the correct URL themselves.

questions.stem: What is the primary purpose of a firewall?

choices.A: To encrypt data stored on a hard drive
choices.B: To monitor and filter network traffic based on security rules
choices.C: To scan emails for viruses before delivery
choices.D: To create backups of important files

questions.explanation: A firewall monitors incoming and outgoing network traffic and blocks anything that does not meet predefined security rules. It acts as a barrier between a trusted internal network and untrusted external networks.
questions.tip: Firewalls filter traffic — they do not encrypt data, scan emails, or create backups. Those are functions of other security tools.

questions.stem: Which statement correctly distinguishes the internet from the World Wide Web?

choices.A: The internet and the WWW are different names for the same thing
choices.B: The WWW is the global network of interconnected computers; the internet is a collection of web pages
choices.C: The internet is the global network infrastructure; the WWW is a service that runs on top of it
choices.D: The internet uses HTTP; the WWW uses TCP/IP

questions.explanation: The internet is the physical and logical infrastructure — the global network of interconnected computers. The WWW is one service that runs on top of the internet, consisting of web pages accessed via HTTP/HTTPS. Email, FTP, and online gaming also use the internet but are not part of the WWW.
questions.tip: This is one of the most common Paper 1 traps. Think of it this way: the internet is the road; the WWW is one type of vehicle that travels on it.

questions.stem: What does a DNS server do when a user types a URL into a browser?

choices.A: It encrypts the connection between the browser and the web server
choices.B: It translates the domain name into an IP address
choices.C: It stores a copy of the web page for faster loading
choices.D: It checks whether the website is safe to visit

questions.explanation: DNS (Domain Name System) translates human-readable domain names (www.example.com) into IP addresses that computers use to identify each other on the network. Without DNS, users would need to remember IP addresses instead of domain names.
questions.tip: Two steps for a full marks answer: (1) the device sends a request to the DNS server with the domain name, (2) the DNS server responds with the corresponding IP address.

questions.stem: An employee receives an email appearing to be from their bank, asking them to click a link and verify their account details. The link leads to a fake website. Which attack is this?
questions.explanation: This is a phishing attack. The attacker sends a fraudulent email that appears legitimate, containing a link to a fake website designed to steal credentials. The attack relies on the victim trusting the email and clicking the link.
questions.tip: Phishing always involves a deceptive email and a fake link or website. The victim must choose to click something — distinguishing it from pharming, where no click is needed.

questions.stem: Why was IPv6 introduced to replace IPv4?

choices.B: IPv4 addresses ran out — there were not enough unique addresses for all devices
choices.C: IPv6 is more secure than IPv4
choices.D: IPv4 could not support wireless connections

questions.explanation: IPv4 uses 32-bit addresses, supporting approximately 4.3 billion unique addresses. With the explosion of internet-connected devices, this number became insufficient. IPv6 uses 128-bit addresses, providing an effectively unlimited number of unique addresses.
questions.tip: The reason is address exhaustion — not speed, security, or wireless capability. State that IPv4 is 32-bit (4.3 billion addresses) and IPv6 is 128-bit.

questions.stem: A network administrator wants to identify security vulnerabilities before attackers can exploit them. Which method involves authorised professionals attempting to break into the system using real attack techniques?
questions.explanation: Penetration testing involves authorised security professionals attempting to breach a system using the same techniques a real attacker would use. The goal is to discover vulnerabilities and fix them before malicious actors can exploit them.
questions.tip: Penetration testing = authorised, simulated attack. Code reviews find vulnerabilities in source code. Audit trails log access — they detect breaches after the fact, not before.

questions.stem: A student reads a URL: http://www.school.com/files/notes.pdf. What is the name of the document being retrieved?
questions.explanation: The document name is notes.pdf — the filename at the end of the path after the last forward slash. The structure of a URL is: protocol (http://) → domain (www.school.com) → path (/files/) → document (notes.pdf).
questions.tip: Pearson asks for the filename only, not the full path. '/files/notes.pdf' would not score the mark — only 'notes.pdf' is accepted.

questions.stem: Which device is responsible for connecting a home network to the internet by directing traffic between the local network and the ISP?
questions.explanation: A router directs data packets between different networks — specifically between the local home network (LAN) and the internet via the ISP. It assigns IP addresses to devices on the local network and manages all traffic in and out.
questions.tip: Common confusion: a modem connects your network to the ISP signal; a router then directs traffic between your LAN and the internet. A switch connects devices within the LAN. A WAP adds wireless connectivity.
